edges.description: Loss of enzyme activity allows GM1 ganglioside to accumulate in lysosomes.
edges.source: GLB1 Loss of Function and Beta-Galactosidase Deficiency
edges.target: Lysosomal GM1 Ganglioside Accumulation in Neurons

edges.description: Lysosomal storage impairs neuronal physiology and drives neurodegeneration.
edges.source: Lysosomal GM1 Ganglioside Accumulation in Neurons

edges.description: Progressive neuronal storage and dysfunction cause loss of acquired developmental milestones.
Somatic lysosomal storage contributes to the skeletal dysplasia/dysostosis multiplex phenotype.
Somatic storage contributes to coarse facial features.
Retinal ganglion-cell storage produces the macular cherry-red spot.
edges.target: Cherry red spot of the macula
GLB1 Loss of Function and Beta-Galactosidase Deficiency

nodes.description: A macular cherry-red spot reflects perifoveal ganglion-cell storage.
nodes.id: Cherry red spot of the macula

evidence.snippet: infants frequently have accompanying hepatosplenomegaly, skeletal dysplasia, cherry-red maculae, cardiomyopathy, and coarse facial features

nodes.description: Coarse facies develop as somatic storage progresses.

evidence.snippet: infants frequently have accompanying hepatosplenomegaly, skeletal dysplasia, cherry-red maculae, cardiomyopathy, and coarse facial features

nodes.description: Loss of acquired developmental milestones in infancy.

evidence.reference_title: The natural history of Type 1 infantile GM1 gangliosidosis: A literature-based meta-analysis.
evidence.snippet:
almost all patients experienced significant multi-organ system
dysfunction and neurodevelopmental regression, particularly in the 6- to
18-month

nodes.description: A characteristic constellation of skeletal dysplasia (dysostosis multiplex).

evidence.snippet: infants frequently have accompanying hepatosplenomegaly, skeletal dysplasia, cherry-red maculae, cardiomyopathy, and coarse facial features

meta.association: Biallelic loss-of-function GLB1 variants causing beta-galactosidase deficiency

evidence.snippet: GM1 gangliosidosis is a progressive, neurosomatic, lysosomal storage disorder caused by mutations in the GLB1 gene encoding the enzyme β-galactosidase.

nodes.description: Biallelic loss-of-function variants in GLB1 abolish lysosomal beta-galactosidase activity. The enzyme normally cleaves the terminal beta-1,4-linked galactose from GM1 ganglioside; its loss is the primary biochemical lesion.
nodes.id: GLB1 Loss of Function and Beta-Galactosidase Deficiency

evidence.snippet: Mutation of the GLB1 gene, which codes for β-gal, prevents cleavage of the terminal β-1,4-linked galactose residue from GM1 ganglioside.

nodes.description: Visceral organomegaly from somatic storage is characteristic of infantile GM1.

evidence.snippet: infants frequently have accompanying hepatosplenomegaly, skeletal dysplasia, cherry-red maculae, cardiomyopathy, and coarse facial features

nodes.description: GM1 ganglioside and other beta-galactose-terminal glycoconjugates accumulate in neuronal lysosomes (and in somatic tissues), impairing cell physiology.
nodes.id: Lysosomal GM1 Ganglioside Accumulation in Neurons

evidence.snippet: Absent or reduced β-galactosidase activity leads to the accumulation of β-linked galactose-containing glycoconjugates including the glycosphingolipid (GSL) GM1-ganglioside in neuronal tissue.

nodes.description: Rapidly progressive neurodegeneration with loss of nervous-system function.

evidence.snippet: precipitates dysfunction of the nervous system

nodes.description: Accumulated substrate impairs cell physiology and precipitates progressive dysfunction of the nervous system, together with somatic (visceral, skeletal) storage pathology.

evidence.snippet: Subsequent accumulation of GM1 ganglioside and other substrates in the lysosome impairs cell physiology and precipitates dysfunction of the nervous system.
